sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 128, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Need to position both greens at frames with correct colors, then click center to submit. Let me move right green to right frame.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 127 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 4.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The right green stayed at 30-32 even after 2 RIGHT actions. So the right green is blocked and can't move right past the bridge. This confirms the right green can only move within the left frame and bridge area.
Current state:
- Left green: cols 12-14, center c (at left c-square)
- Right green: cols 30-32, center W (at bridge, can't move right)
- Bar: need to check
The right green is stuck at the bridge. So the right green's target position must be the bridge (cols 30-32).
Now I need to figure out the correct center color configuration. Currently:
- Left green: center c
- Right green: center W
I've tried clicking center with this configuration and it didn't work. Let me try the other configuration:
- Left green: center W
- Right green: center c
To get this, I need to click either green (which swaps centers). Let me click the right green to swap centers, then click center.
But wait, I also need to consider the bar value. The bar might need to be at a specific value for the level to complete. Let me check the bar value.
Actually, let me step back and think about this more carefully. I've been trying random configurations without a clear hypothesis. Let me try to systematically explore the possible configurations:
1. Left green position: can be anywhere in the left frame (cols 10-25)
2. Right green position: can be anywhere in the left frame and bridge (cols 10-32)
3. Left green center: c or W
4. Right green center: c or W
5. Bar value: 0-64
The possible configurations are:
- Left green at left c-square (12-14), right green at bridge (30-32)
  - Centers: c, c (need to swap)
  - Centers: c, W (current)
  - Centers: W, c (need to swap)
  - Centers: W, W (need to swap twice)
I've tried c, W and it didn't work. Let me try c, c.
To get c, c:
- Current: left: c, right: W
- Click right green: swap → left: W, right: c
- Now I have W, c, not c, c
Hmm, clicking swaps centers, so I can't get c, c from c, W by clicking. Unless... the center color is determined by position. When the left green is at the left c-square, its center is c. When the right green is at the bridge, its center is W. So the center colors are fixed by position, not by clicking.
Wait, but earlier when I clicked greens, the centers did swap. So clicking does change centers. But maybe the centers also change based on position. For example, when a green enters a c-square, its center becomes c. When a green enters the bridge, its center becomes W.
Let me test this hypothesis: move the left green away from the left c-square and see if its center changes.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me move both greens to the same position (both at the left c-square) and see if their centers become the same.
Or let me try moving the right green to the left c-square as well. If both greens are at the left c-square, maybe both centers become c.
Let me try: LEFT the right green to move it to the left c-square.
Current right green: cols 30-32. To move to cols 12-14, I need to move left by 18 columns = 6 LEFT actions.
But wait, the right green is selected. So LEFT would move the right green. Let me try 6 LEFTs.
Actually, that would also change the bar. Let me just try a few LEFTs and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: Right green stuck at bridge (can't move right). Let me try moving right green LEFT into the left frame to the c-square position, then click center.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Right green at 30-32, need to move to 12-14 (left c-square)
# That's 18 columns left = 6 LEFT actions
# Right green is selected

# Do 6 LEFTs
result = action(['LEFT'] * 6)
print("After 6 LEFTs:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"  Green: boundary={n['boundary']}")
        for c in n['children']:
            cn = [x for x in seg['nodes'] if x['id'] == c][0]
            print(f"    Center: color={cn['color']}")

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"  Bar: pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Now click center
result2 = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 32}])
print(f"\nAfter clicking center: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After 6 LEFTs:
  Green: boundary=[[30, 9], [30, 11], [32, 11], [32, 9]]
    Center: color=c
  Green: boundary=[[30, 15], [30, 17], [32, 17], [32, 15]]
    Center: color=W

After clicking center: board_changed=True, level_completed=False
